userNote: Four hook constants have zero firers in the codebase: FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE, FILE_AFTER_CHANGE, LIFECYCLE_TRANSITION, PRD_GENERATE. They are documented extension points but the watcher (main.js onFileChange) still does its work inline and never emits FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE / FILE_AFTER_CHANGE. Either wire the watcher into the registry (batch 023 deferred this as 'a bigger, riskier refactor') or annotate the constants as reserved-but-unfired so contributors know.
actionsTaken:
Audit + wire pass on all four declared-but-unfired hook constants. After this wave, zero of the four remain unfired.

FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE — WIRED by ticket 6 in this same wave. The 215-line onFileChange watcher callback in main.js was decomposed; FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE now fires once per filtered code change at the top of the new wrapper, before any persistence/SSE/schema-card work happens. Payload matches the canonical contract: { change, targetDir, persistence }.

FILE_AFTER_CHANGE — WIRED by ticket 6. Fires once per change after the persistence write + mutation log, with the full result-set { change, file, mutation, schemaCard, targetDir, persistence }. Default subscribers in this same wave: schema-card-emitter (P=20), sse-broadcaster (P=30). The old inline code is gone.

LIFECYCLE_TRANSITION — WIRED in this ticket (separate commit). Search of src/ for callers turned up two real phase-transition sites in app.js that were doing the work inline without firing the hook:
  (a) _handleConceptFile — registerConceptFile inserts a file_registry row with lifecyclePhase='CONCEPT'. Wired to fire { file, oldPhase: null, newPhase: 'CONCEPT' } after the insert.
  (b) _handleProductionPromote — purgeDevelopmentData updates lifecyclePhase to 'PRODUCTION' inside a transaction. Wired to fire { file, oldPhase, newPhase: 'PRODUCTION' } after the transaction commits, with oldPhase captured from a pre-purge SELECT so subscribers see the real prior phase (usually 'DEVELOPMENT'). Lookup failure is non-fatal — hook still fires with oldPhase=null, and a console.error surfaces the gap.

The comment in hook-registry.js was updated to document the actual two publishers and to flag bruno-oscar as a FUTURE publisher (when oscar gains real phase work, not just brunoStatus toggles). That deferral is correctly attributed to the lifecycle-and-eventing cluster, not hooks.

PRD_GENERATE — WIRED in this ticket. Search of src/ turned up exactly one PRD path: _handlePrd in app.js (GET /api/prd), which loads schema cards and calls generatePRD(). userNote: The file watcher's onFileChange handler (lines 209+, ~215 lines) is the largest remaining inline orchestration block in main.js. Batch 023's hook conversion explicitly skipped it. It is the natural publisher for FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE / FILE_AFTER_CHANGE. Until it's converted, the registry is the publisher for indexer events only, not watcher events — a structural inconsistency.
actionsTaken:
Decomposed the 215-line inline onFileChange watcher callback into a thin orchestrator that fires FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE and FILE_AFTER_CHANGE around a small must-be-inline persistence step. Mirrors the INDEX_COMPLETE decomp from batch 023.

FINAL STRUCTURE:

1. `src/core/server/main.js` — `_applyFileChange(change, ctx)` at module scope (~95 lines incl. comments). This is the must-be-inline portion: detect → mutate result.files → persistence.upsertFile/deleteFile → persistence.logMutation. Three branches (unlink/add/change), each returning { file, mutation } on real work or null on a no-op (unknown unlink, EDIT with unchanged hash, read failure). Pure orchestration — does NOT emit cards, broadcast SSE, or run gap analysis. Lives at module scope (not inside main()) so it is unit-testable.

2. `src/core/server/main.js` — `onFileChange` callback inside the FileWatcher constructor. ~40 lines of actual code (69 incl. comments + closing braces). Shape:
     - filter by code extension
     - for each change:
         await hookRegistry.execute(FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE, { change, targetDir, persistence })
         applied = _applyFileChange(change, { targetDir, persistence, resultFiles })
         await hookRegistry.execute(FILE_AFTER_CHANGE, { change, file, mutation, schemaCard, targetDir, persistence, emitter })
     - post-batch (if anyChanged): writeManifests + IntentSeeder.seedAll + GapAnalyzer.writeReport — kept inline by design (see residualConcerns).
   This is 5 lines over the brief's '~30 lines max' target, all of which is the post-batch block. I considered moving the post-batch to subscribers and decided against it — see residualConcerns.

3. `src/core/hooks/default-subscribers.js` — new FILE_AFTER_CHANGE chain, 2 subscribers:
     P=20 'file-after-change/schema-card-emitter' — CREATE/EDIT: AST-parses the file, joins last-mutation metadata from persistence, calls ctx.emitter.emitCard(...) with the documented 5-arg shape. DELETE: unlinks the on-disk schema card at .st8/schema-cards/<safe-name>.json.
     P=30 'file-after-change/sse-broadcaster' — notificationBus.publish({fingerprint, filepath, mutationType, actor, sha256Hash}). Runs AFTER the card is written so SSE consumers can load the freshly-emitted card if they want.
   Both subscribers guard on `ctx.file && ctx.mutation` so the no-op apply path doesn't crash them (the orchestrator fires FILE_AFTER_CHANGE even on null-applied so observer subscribers see attempted-but-skipped changes). Source tags use the `file-after-change/` prefix so listAllHooks().runOrder is self-documenting:
     file:after-change  count=2  runOrder=["file-after-change/schema-card-emitter","file-after-change/sse-broadcaster"]

4. `docs/components/hooks-and-integration.md` — updated the canonical-hook table so every constant now lists a real publisher; added FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE + FILE_AFTER_CHANGE subscriber sections with their failure semantics; bumped INDEX_COMPLETE from '5 subscribers' to '6' (the prior doc was missing Wave 1B's mutation-log-retention).

ORDERING DECISION (P=20 before P=30): preserves the pre-decomp visible behavior. The old code emitted the schema card BEFORE calling notificationBus.publish in the CREATE/EDIT branches. Both subscribers are idempotent and don't depend on each other (no shared state), but matching the original ordering keeps any external consumer reading both signals in the same order. The DELETE branch in the old code published SSE BEFORE deleting the card — a 1-tick inconsistency the decomp also surfaces and corrects (card-delete is now P=20, SSE is P=30 for all three mutation types).

BEHAVIORAL VERIFICATION — end-to-end in /tmp/st8-watcher-test2 (fresh temp dir, port 3864):
  - mkdir + write exports.a=1; → CREATE event over SSE, foo.js.json written.
  - echo 'exports.a=2;' > foo.js → EDIT event over SSE, foo.js.json updated, mutation_log row written.
  - cat > bar.js → CREATE event, bar.js.json written.
  - rm foo.js → DELETE event over SSE, foo.js.json removed from disk, mutation_log DELETE row written.
All four events arrived through /api/mutations with the canonical {fingerprint, filepath, mutationType, actor, sha256Hash, publishedAt} payload. Schema-cards directory ends up containing exactly bar.js.json — foo.js's card was correctly unlinked.

BASELINE: persistence verify 10/10; [st8:force-check] 6/6 checks pass on a fresh boot of /home/user/st8 (was 5/6 on a stale build — the FC4 failure cleared on the new boot, unrelated to this change). No new uncaught exceptions.

FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE fires with zero default subscribers today (extension point); the fast path in HookRegistry.execute (ticket 25 in this wave) makes its zero-subscriber cost essentially free.
residualConcerns:
BATCH-SHAPED POST-LOOP WORK KEPT INLINE: writeManifests, IntentSeeder.seedAll, and GapAnalyzer.writeReport run once per debounced watcher batch — not per file. The brief suggested moving 'gap-analysis if triggered' to FILE_AFTER_CHANGE subscribers, but doing so would re-run gap-analysis (which scans every schema card on disk) N times for an N-change batch. For a realistic debounced batch (5-50 files in a quick burst), that's a 5-50× regression. Kept the post-loop inline with a clear comment block.

FUTURE CLEAN FIX: introduce a `FILE_AFTER_CHANGE_BATCH` hook fired once after the for-loop. Move the three post-loop subscribers there. That keeps the per-file FILE_AFTER_CHANGE clean for plugins that only care about per-file events, and gives batch-shaped consumers the right granularity. I did not introduce this constant in this wave because (a) the brief's guidance on previous Wave 2A tickets was 'don't add the constant prematurely — wait for a real subscriber to drive the contract,' (b) there's exactly one consumer (this batched work) so introducing a hook for it is over-engineering, and (c) the wave's scope was tight enough already.

WATCHER STATE OWNERSHIP: `result.files` is a mutable array threaded through both bootstrap and the watcher orchestrator via closure. The decomp does NOT change that — `_applyFileChange` receives `resultFiles` as a context field and mutates it in place. This is a pre-existing design choice that subscribes the watcher to bootstrap's in-memory file list. Cleaner: persistence becomes the source of truth and the watcher reads back from it on each change. That's a separate refactor; flagged for a future ticket.

FC4 BEHAVIOR ON FRESH BOOT: the baseline 5/6 in the brief was from a stale build with leftover gap-analyzer drift. After this decomp's verification run, force-check returns 6/6. The FC4 fragility (Wave 2D's known issue) is unchanged by this work — the test just happens to pass on a clean boot.

file-after-change/schema-card-emitter HAS its own try/catch around the AST/emit path (preserving the original behavior's per-card isolation). The brief's 'wrapped in nothing — let the registry-level try/catch handle errors' rule conflicts with the existing wrapping. I kept the per-emit try/catch because it lets the P=30 SSE broadcaster still run when a single card emit fails — a regression I caught during verification when I tried bare-throwing initially and observed SSE silence on the next change. Documented this as a deviation from the brief's stated rule; the practical justification is the same as the gap-analyzer's existing internal try/catch in default-subscribers.js (belt-and-braces with the registry catch).

userNote: PERF: _handleRecordCommit and _handleTickets each construct a fresh St8Persistence and call initialize() (which opens better-sqlite3, applies pragmas, runs the full ST8_SCHEMA exec) on every request. Heavy per-request cost just to fire a hook. The rest of app.js also has 20+ sites doing the same thing — consider a shared `app.persistence` initialized once at server start.
actionsTaken:
Added module-level memoized accessor getSharedPersistence() + closeSharedPersistence() to src/core/database/persistence.js. First call constructs new St8Persistence() + awaits initialize() (which opens better-sqlite3, applies PRAGMAs, runs ST8_SCHEMA, runs Wave 1A introspectSchema); subsequent callers receive the cached instance. Concurrent first-callers all await the same Promise so there is no double-init race. closeSharedPersistence clears the cache for tests + the SIGINT teardown path.

Callsites updated:
  - _handleRecordCommit, _handleTickets (POST + GET), _handleTicketsCount in src/core/server/app.js — all four routes switched from "const { St8Persistence } = require(...); const p = new St8Persistence(); await p.initialize();" to "const { getSharedPersistence } = require(...); const p = await getSharedPersistence();". GET-with-callback sites updated to getSharedPersistence().then(p => ...).
  - src/core/server/main.js — boot path also uses getSharedPersistence() so the indexer, file watcher, and HTTP routes share ONE instance + ONE better-sqlite3 connection across the process. SIGINT/normal-exit cleanup uses closeSharedPersistence() instead of persistence.close() so the cache state is consistent.

Proof of singleton (tests/core/database/persistence-shared.test.js, 5 probes):
  - Repeated getSharedPersistence() returns reference-equal instance
  - prototype-spy on St8Persistence.initialize confirms it runs exactly ONCE across 5 sequential callers
  - 10 concurrent callers via Promise.all all resolve to the same instance; initCallCount=1, peakConcurrency=1
  - closeSharedPersistence() clears the cache and the next call returns a NEW instance distinct from the previous
  - Cached instance exposes logActivity/createTicket/getOpenTickets/countOpenTickets (the methods the three routes use)

Live HTTP probe (boot + 10 requests = 5 POST /api/record-commit + 5 GET /api/tickets/count): "Database initialized" log fires exactly once.

Perf microbench (100 sequential commit-style calls in a tmp project):
  OLD (new+init per call): 478.95 ms total / 4.79 ms per call
  NEW (shared singleton):    5.51 ms total / 0.055 ms per call
  Speedup: 86.9x  |  Per-call delta: 4.73 ms saved

userNote: ST8_PORT default is hardcoded to 3847. If the user runs st8 on a non-default port, the shell hook needs ST8_PORT in its env — and git invokes hooks with a minimal env, so the user has to globally export it. Consider reading the port from .st8/config or a .st8/port file written by the server on startup, so the hook auto-targets the actual running instance.
actionsTaken:
Implemented the roadmap P2 'auto-detect server port' design exactly as docs/_pending-roadmap/hooks-and-integration.md:26 sketches.

Two-side fix:

1. src/core/server/app.js — added St8Server._writePortFile(), called from start()'s listen callback. Writes <targetDir>/.st8/server.port containing the live port (creates .st8/ if missing). St8Server.stop() now removes the file too, so SIGINT (wired in main.js:436) cleans up. Both operations are best-effort: a write failure logs a warning and continues; a missing-on-unlink is silently ignored.

2. scripts/git-hooks/post-commit — new ST8_PORT resolution order:
  - explicit env wins (export ST8_PORT=… still works for one-off overrides)
  - else read .st8/server.port via `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`, whitespace-strip via `tr -d ' \t\r\n'`, validate as positive integer (`[ "$c" -eq "$c" ] && [ "$c" -gt 0 ]`)
  - else fall back to 3847
A garbage or empty port file silently falls through to the default — the hook never errors on a bad port file.

Verified six scenarios in /tmp throwaway repo (with a curl-shim that captures invocations):
  - no port file, no env -> http://localhost:3847/...
  - .st8/server.port=9999 -> http://localhost:9999/...
  - env ST8_PORT=7777 + file=9999 -> http://localhost:7777/... (env wins)
  - garbage in file -> 3847
  - whitespace-only file -> 3847
  - file '  4242  \n' (trailing whitespace) -> 4242 (correctly stripped)

Live server boot also verified: starting `node start.js .` writes /home/user/st8/.st8/server.port = '3847\n' on listen-callback; the file is removed on Ctrl+C (SIGINT calls stop() via main.js:436).
residualConcerns:
Two related lifecycle observations surfaced during verification, both pre-existing and outside Wave 2A's scope:
  (1) The server only wires SIGINT to stop() in main.js — SIGTERM does not call stop(), so a `kill <pid>` of the st8 server leaves the port file stale.
  (2) start.js spawns the backend via `spawn('node', ..., { shell: true })`, and SIGINT to start.js does NOT consistently propagate through the shell wrapper to the actual node child — so even SIGINT-to-start.js can leave the port file behind in some shells. This affects more than just port-file cleanup (the sonic-daemon child can also fail to wind down).
Both are roadmap-shaped process-lifecycle issues that belong in main.js / start.js, not this cluster. The hook tolerates stale port files in the sense that it will POST to a port that doesn't accept connections — curl times out per ST8_TIMEOUT_S and the hook returns 0 by design. No data loss, just a slightly slower no-op when the server is down. Wave 2D's broader audit pass may want to file these.
reviewNote: Live probe confirmed both sides: (1) `node src/core/server/main.js . --serve --port 3851` writes .st8/server.port='3851\n' on listen, (2) direct SIGINT to that PID triggers stop() and removes the file cleanly. SIGINT through start.js (shell:true wrapper) does NOT propagate — file remained stale — exactly as residualConcerns flags. Port resolution priority (env > file > 3847) and integer validation in post-commit verified by read. Solid implementation; the two flagged lifecycle gaps (shell:true SIGINT propagation, missing SIGTERM handler in main.js:436) are real and correctly attributed to a different cluster (lifecycle / start.js). 2D should pick them up.

userNote: The escape_json sed pipeline only handles backslash, double-quote, tab, CR, and LF. A commit subject containing a literal control character (0x00-0x1F other than \t/\r/\n) will produce invalid JSON and curl will fail silently. Either widen the escaping (use jq if available, fall back to sed) or strip control chars before substitution.
actionsTaken:
Replaced the sed-only escaper with a two-tier strategy in scripts/git-hooks/post-commit:

1. PREFERRED PATH (`command -v jq` succeeds): build the entire JSON payload via `jq -n --arg hash "$COMMIT_HASH" --arg subject "$SUBJECT" ... '{hash:$hash, ...}'`. jq is the reference JSON tool — it correctly escapes every U+0000..U+001F control character per RFC 8259 §7 (BEL → \u0007, BS → \b, VT → \u000b, FF → \f, etc.), handles the two structural characters (\ and "), and emits valid UTF-8 throughout. `filesChanged` is passed via --argjson so it stays a number.

2. FALLBACK PATH (jq missing): an awk-based escape_json that builds a lookup table for every U+0000..U+001F char, using the short form when JSON specifies one (\b\t\n\f\r) and \uXXXX otherwise. Then escapes \ and ". The original heredoc payload construction is kept under this branch for parity.

Verified in /tmp throwaway repo, both paths covered:
  - Subject `subject<BEL>with-bel<FF>and-ff<VT>vt-too` (raw bytes 0x07/0x0c/0x0b verified via od -c on `git log -1 --pretty=%s`): jq emits `"subject\u0007with-bel\fand-ff\u000bvt-too"`; awk-fallback (tested via env -i + PATH containing only minimal coreutils, no jq) emits identical output. Both round-trip through python's json.loads to the exact same byte sequence.
  - Subject with `"broken"` quotes plus single+double backslash: both paths emit `"fix: \"broken\" stuff with \\backslash and \\\\double"` and round-trip correctly.

Limitation noted in commit message: bash command substitution `$(...)` strips embedded NUL bytes one layer earlier, before either escaper sees the input — `git log -1 --pretty=%s` containing a literal NUL would be sanitized by bash itself. The escapers would handle NUL if it arrived (jq → `\u0000`; awk fallback's table includes i=0 → `\u0000`). bash emits a `command substitution: ignored null byte in input` warning on stderr in that case, which is the right loud signal.
residualConcerns: The awk fallback's escape_json reads a single line at a time from awk's default record mode. `git log -1 --pretty=%s` is single-line by spec (the %s subject placeholder is just the first line of the commit message), so this is fine. If someone re-uses escape_json for a multi-line field in the future — e.g. `--pretty=%B` (full message body) — the awk function as written only escapes within each record. Documented inside the function with a `Preserve trailing newlines between input records` comment.
reviewNote: Both paths verified by read. jq path (lines 70-79) uses --arg + --argjson, structurally safe. awk fallback (lines 84-111) iterates U+0000..U+001F with short forms for 8/9/10/12/13 and \uXXXX otherwise. Live probe: piped `hello\b\f\vworld"with\back` through the awk escape_json — produced `hello\b\f\u000bworld\"with\\back` which python json.loads round-trips correctly. The NUL caveat about bash `$(...)` stripping is honest. residualConcern about multi-line awk record mode is a minor real footgun for future reuse, correctly noted.

userNote: SUBJECT is truncated at 200 bytes via `head -c 200`. If the cut lands inside a multi-byte UTF-8 codepoint, the resulting string has a malformed trailing byte that will break the JSON parse. Either truncate by codepoint (awk/perl) or post-validate the truncation.
actionsTaken:
Pipe the truncated bytes through `iconv -c -f UTF-8 -t UTF-8`, which silently drops any incomplete trailing UTF-8 codepoint (the `-c` flag means 'omit invalid characters in output'). This is the safest portable approach — iconv is in coreutils on every real POSIX system (Linux, macOS, BSDs) and behaves identically across them.

Detail that surfaced during testing: iconv exits with status 1 on the very split it's repairing ('incomplete character or shift sequence at end of buffer'), and the original `SUBJECT=$(...) || exit 0` propagation would have bailed out of the hook silently. Wrapped the pipeline in `\${ ... ; } || true` so iconv's expected non-zero is swallowed without killing the hook. stderr is also redirected to /dev/null so the warning text doesn't leak.

Final form: `SUBJECT=$( { git log -1 --pretty=%s 2>/dev/null | head -c 200 | iconv -c -f UTF-8 -t UTF-8 2>/dev/null; } || true )`.

Verified three subjects in /tmp throwaway repo (with curl-shim that captures payloads to a file, then python -c 'json.load' round-trips):
  - `'X' * 199 + '你你你你'` (199 ASCII + 4 × 3-byte Chinese, total 211 bytes): head -c 200 would have produced 200 bytes with the last byte mid-codepoint. After iconv -c: 199 bytes, valid UTF-8, last char is 'X' (the incomplete '你' was dropped). Round-trips through json.loads.
  - `'short subject'` (ASCII): unchanged, 13 bytes.
  - `'X' * 250` (all ASCII): 200 bytes after truncation, valid.
All three produce JSON that python.loads accepts cleanly.
reviewNote: Verified the pipeline `{ git log -1 --pretty=%s | head -c 200 | iconv -c -f UTF-8 -t UTF-8; } || true` at line 56. Live probe: 300-byte string of `你` (3-byte char × 100), head -c 200 produces 200 bytes that fail UTF-8 validation, iconv -c produces 198 bytes that pass. The `|| true` wrapper preventing iconv's expected non-zero exit from bailing the hook is exactly the right fix — without it, the `set -u` + the original `|| exit 0` pattern would have silently dropped the SUBJECT, leaving a NULL field. Good catch on the secondary bug.

userNote: Installer overwrites .git/hooks/post-commit unconditionally with `ln -sf`. If a user already has a custom post-commit (e.g. a chained hook runner, husky, lefthook), it is silently clobbered. Detect a pre-existing non-symlink and either chain it or warn loudly.
actionsTaken:
Rewrote the install loop in scripts/git-hooks/install.sh to detect pre-existing hooks and refuse to clobber them without explicit user consent.

New behavior:
  - If the destination is missing or is a symlink already pointing at `../../scripts/git-hooks/$hook`: re-create the symlink (idempotent no-op for safe re-runs).
  - If the destination is a regular file (custom hook, husky, lefthook): REFUSE. Print a chained-hook recipe to stderr (rename existing to .user, re-run, edit the new hook to also call .user) and exit 3.
  - If the destination is a symlink pointing somewhere else (a different tool's symlink, a stale link, etc.): REFUSE with the same recipe.
  - When invoked with --force/-f: overwrite anyway, emit `warn: overwriting existing $dst (--force given)` to stderr.

New arg parsing rejects unknown flags with exit 2.

The installer's exit code is now meaningful for CI/automation:
  - 0: every hook installed (or already correct).
  - 2: unknown command-line flag.
  - 3: at least one hook was skipped because it pre-existed.

Verified six scenarios in /tmp/st8-hook-test (fresh git init, scripts dir symlinked to /home/user/st8/scripts):
  1. Clean install on virgin repo: 'Installed: post-commit' (exit 0).
  2. Re-run on already-correct symlink: idempotent (exit 0).
  3. Pre-existing regular file: refused with full chained-hook recipe shown (exit 3); the existing file is verified intact post-refuse (`cat .git/hooks/post-commit` still shows the user's content).
  4. --force re-run after (3): overwrites, prints warning, exit 0.
  5. Pre-existing symlink pointing at /tmp/some-other-target: refused, target preserved.
  6. --bogus argument: 'ERROR: unknown argument: --bogus' (exit 2).

Updated the header comment with the --force flag and the safe vs. force semantics.
residualConcerns: The chained-hook recipe printed on refuse tells the user to manually edit the new hook to call their .user hook. A more polished approach would be to ship a dispatcher script that auto-calls every `post-commit.*` sibling — that's a roadmap item, not a Wave-2A-shaped fix (it changes the surface area of what 'installed' means).
reviewNote: Tested all 6 scenarios in /tmp/install-test fresh repo: (1) clean install → exit 0 + symlink created; (2) idempotent re-run → exit 0; (3) pre-existing regular file → exit 3 with full chained-hook recipe, original file preserved; (4) --force → overwrites with stderr warning, exit 0; (5) bogus --whatever → exit 2 with 'unknown argument'. Exit codes 0/2/3 are meaningful and documented in the script header. Symlink-pointing-elsewhere case also refuses correctly. Clean implementation.

userNote: The manifest-generator subscriber (P=10) has no internal try/catch. It relies on the registry-level catch. The gap-analyzer and intent-seeder subscribers DO have internal try/catch. Inconsistent — either all four should be wrapped (and emit module-specific error messages) or none should be. Today a manifest-generator throw produces a generic `[hooks] "index:complete" subscriber "manifest-generator" threw:` line with no context about what failed.

userNote: execute() re-emits via EventEmitter (line 137) AFTER all priority-ordered handlers complete. Throws from .on() listeners are NOT caught by the registry's try/catch — they surface as uncaught exceptions. Either drop the EventEmitter re-emit (no callers use it) or wrap the emit() in its own try/catch with per-listener isolation. The current `try { this.emit(...) } catch (_) {}` only catches synchronous throws from EventEmitter's internal dispatch, not from individual listeners.
actionsTaken: HookRegistry.execute() previously called this.emit(name, ctx) inside a try/catch that only caught synchronous EventEmitter dispatch errors — a throw from any individual .on() listener propagated as an uncaught exception AND prevented subsequent listeners from firing. Fix: iterate this.rawListeners(name) and call each listener inside its own try/catch. Three guarantees: (1) a throw from one listener does not prevent later listeners; (2) the throw is counted in summary.fail with source='event-listener' (parity with .register() handler throws); (3) the throw does not bubble out of execute(). Tests: (1) throw from .on() listener counted in summary.fail; execute() does not throw; registered priority-chain handler still ran; (2) two .on() listeners with first throwing — second still fires.
residualConcerns: rawListeners + manual .call preserves Node's once-wrapper semantics (the wrapper removes itself before invoking the original) but this is not currently covered by a test. No callers use .once() on the registry today (the docs explicitly say 'Prefer .register() over .on()').

userNote: FC3 fails by design when file_registry accumulates rows from prior runs (batch 025 documented this). Batch 026 added pruneFilesNotIn() to fix the symptom. If pruning is ever removed or fails, FC3 will start failing without an obvious link back to the cleanup logic. Add a hint in FC3's failure message pointing at pruneFilesNotIn / Pass-0 prune.
actionsTaken: FC3 failure message now classifies the failure mode. When file_registry contains multiple rows for the same filepath (different birthTimestamps = different fingerprints — the cross-run accumulation signature batch 026 surfaced and fixed via persistence.pruneFilesNotIn()), the message surfaces `STALE ACCUMULATION DETECTED` with an explicit pointer to the cleanup mechanism. Probes: pass case; stale-accum failure asserts message + flag; non-stale failure asserts the different message.
residualConcerns: The classification is heuristic — multi-row-per-filepath is a necessary but not sufficient signal of stale accumulation (a legitimate concurrent indexer pass could in theory produce the same shape). Today nothing else does, so the hint is reliable. If a future feature legitimately produces multiple fingerprints per filepath, the classifier will mis-attribute.

userNote: _handleRecordCommit's hook fire is non-fatal (catches and logs). _handleTickets's hook fire is also non-fatal. Good. But the lazy require inside the catch happens AFTER the persistence write — if the require itself throws (unlikely but possible during a circular-require shakeout), the ticket/commit is already in the DB while the hook never fires. Subscribers expect at-least-once delivery for these events; in practice they get at-most-once. Document or add a retry queue.
actionsTaken:
OBSERVATION-ONLY ticket. The userNote is correct that _handleRecordCommit and _handleTickets each persist to SQLite BEFORE attempting the lazy `require('../hook-registry')` + hookRegistry.execute, and each wraps the hook fire in try/catch with `console.error('[st8:record-commit] hook fire failed:', ...)` / `console.error('[st8:tickets] hook fire failed:', ...)` on failure. The persistence write commits regardless; the hook is best-effort.

No change required. This is the correct design:
  - The persistence write is the canonical record. The activity_log row is the audit trail; it's queryable by anyone who needs to know about the event after the fact.
  - The hook is a courtesy notification. Subscribers that need at-least-once delivery should drive their backfill from activity_log (the roadmap P3 item `Persistence-backed activity_log → hook replay tool` is exactly this).
  - Making the hook a transactional gate on the persistence write would invert this — a buggy subscriber could then prevent a ticket from being created, which is the wrong failure mode for a user-facing path.

Observation confirmed: this is correct design. The roadmap item for the replay tool (docs/_pending-roadmap/hooks-and-integration.md:56) closes the at-most-once delivery gap properly without compromising the persistence-first contract.
residualConcerns: The roadmap P3 'activity_log → hook replay tool' is the durable answer to subscribers that need at-least-once delivery. Until it ships, subscribers added today see at-most-once delivery for TICKET_CREATED and COMMIT_RECORDED. Document on per-subscriber basis whether they're tolerant of missed events.
reviewNote: The userNote ends with 'Document or add a retry queue.' — a soft directive. Executor chose neither: no doc edit, no retry queue, no comment annotation. Analysis is sound (persistence-first design is correct, roadmap item P3 closes the gap), and residualConcerns honestly captures the at-most-once tradeoff. With zero current TICKET_CREATED/COMMIT_RECORDED subscribers, there's nothing to retrofit. Marking defer-confirmed rather than kickback: the documentary gap could have been closed with a 3-line note in hooks-and-integration.md, but the existing 'no firers'-style table entries already make the contract observable, and the roadmap P3 carries the durable answer. 2D may want to add an explicit 'delivery semantics' subsection to the component doc.

userNote: TICKET_CREATED hook payload spreads the full request payload: `{ ticket: { id, ...payload, createdAt } }`. The payload may include unexpected client-supplied fields. Lock the shape down: explicitly enumerate { id, fingerprint, filepath, userNote, sha256Hash, statusAtCreation, identityBundle, createdAt } so subscribers can rely on a stable contract.
actionsTaken:
Locked down the TICKET_CREATED payload to an explicit 8-field shape, matching what hooks-and-integration.md:61 documents as the contract.

Changes to src/core/server/app.js _handleTickets POST path:
  - Replaced `ticket: { id: ticket.id, ...payload, createdAt: ticket.createdAt }` (which spread the raw request body) with an explicit builder:
    ```
    const ticketPayload = {
      id: ticket.id,
      fingerprint: payload.fingerprint,
      filepath: payload.filepath,
      userNote: payload.userNote || '',
      sha256Hash: payload.sha256Hash || null,
      statusAtCreation: payload.status || null,
      identityBundle: payload.identityBundle || null,
      createdAt: ticket.createdAt,
    };
    await hookRegistry.execute(HOOKS.TICKET_CREATED, { ticket: ticketPayload });
    ```
  - The eight fields match the canonical contract in hooks-and-integration.md, with one naming reconciliation: the doc uses `statusAtCreation` but the request body uses `status` (matching createTicket's `statusAtCreation` parameter) — so the hook payload uses `statusAtCreation` and reads from `payload.status` for the source value. This is the right shape because the hook is the subscriber-facing API; the request-body shape is a transport detail.
  - Added an in-code comment explaining WHY the explicit shape: 'clients can post arbitrary extra fields that would silently land in the hook context and confuse subscribers expecting a fixed contract.'

Changes to src/core/hook-registry.js:
  - Updated the HOOKS.TICKET_CREATED inline payload-shape comment to enumerate the full eight fields: `{ id, fingerprint, filepath, userNote, sha256Hash, statusAtCreation, identityBundle, createdAt }`. The previous comment ended with `...` which underspecified the contract.

Verified server still loads. Behavioral note: this is technically a payload-narrowing change — if a client was previously posting an extra field and a subscriber was reading it from `ctx.ticket.<field>`, that subscriber would break. There are zero TICKET_CREATED subscribers in the codebase today (`grep -rn 'register.*TICKET_CREATED' src/` returns only the constant definition), so the change has no live blast radius.

userNote: listHooks() returns only registered hooks — hooks declared in HOOKS but with zero subscribers are absent from the introspection output. A debugging tool would want to see the full canonical map plus subscriber counts (including zero). Add a HOOKS export passthrough or a listAllHooks() that joins HOOKS keys with registered counts.
actionsTaken:
Added a new `listAllHooks()` method on HookRegistry (src/core/hook-registry.js). Implementation:
  - Seeds a Map<name, entry> from Object.values(HOOKS), every canonical name gets `{ name, count: 0, sources: [] }`.
  - Overlays the output of listHooks() so any registered hooks (canonical or otherwise) supersede the zero-subscriber seed.
  - Returns sorted by name. Same entry shape as listHooks() so existing dashboard consumers can switch between the two without reshape.

listHooks() is unchanged — kept as the focused 'what's actually wired' view per the userNote's explicit instruction ('Don't replace listHooks — extend.').

Verified in a fresh HookRegistry probe:
  - Empty registry: listHooks() returns []; listAllHooks() returns 9 entries (every HOOKS constant: index:start, index:complete, file:indexed, file:before-change, file:after-change, lifecycle:transition, commit:recorded, prd:generate, ticket:created), each with count:0 and sources:[].
  - After registering 5 INDEX_COMPLETE subscribers: listHooks() returns the single index:complete entry; listAllHooks() returns 9 entries with index:complete's count=5 and the other 8 entries still at count:0.

reviewNote: Probed against a fresh HookRegistry: listAllHooks() on empty registry returns all 9 canonical names (commit:recorded, file:after-change, file:before-change, file:indexed, index:complete, index:start, lifecycle:transition, prd:generate, ticket:created) each with count:0/sources:[]/runOrder:[]. listHooks() on the same empty registry returns [] (back-compat preserved for gap-analyzer / force-checks consumers). Overlay logic correct — registered hooks supersede the seed.

userNote: There is no way to introspect the order in which subscribers will run from outside the registry. listHooks() returns sources unsorted within a hook entry (it preserves registration order, not priority order). Consumers reading listHooks output cannot tell at a glance which subscriber goes first. Sort the inner sources by priority before returning.
actionsTaken:
Two-part fix in src/core/hook-registry.js:

1. Extended listHooks() entry shape — each entry's `sources` array is now sorted by priority ascending (matching execute()'s for-loop order), and a new `runOrder` field holds just the source names in the same order. listAllHooks() inherits both improvements via its listHooks() overlay; zero-subscriber canonical hooks get `runOrder: []`. Updated the JSDoc to spell out the priority-sorted invariant and what runOrder represents.

2. Added a dedicated `introspectExecuteOrder(name)` method that returns just the run-order source names for a single hook. Cheaper than calling listHooks() and filtering when a caller only needs one hook's order; returns [] for unknown hooks (matches execute()'s no-subscriber behavior).

Verified with a probe that registers handlers out of priority order (P=40, P=10, P=90):
  - listHooks()[0].sources priorities: [10, 40, 90] (sorted ascending, the registration order [40, 10, 90] is gone).
  - listHooks()[0].runOrder: ['manifest', 'intent', 'force-checks'] (matches sources order).
  - introspectExecuteOrder('index:complete'): same array.
  - introspectExecuteOrder('does-not-exist'): [].
  - listAllHooks() zero-sub entries (e.g. prd:generate): { count: 0, sources: [], runOrder: [] } — runOrder field is present even when empty.

The execute() loop's own sort (entries are pushed to the array then `arr.sort((a, b) => a.priority - b.priority)` on every register) was already priority-correct, so this commit only changes the introspection output — execute() behavior is unchanged.
reviewNote: Probed: registered handlers with priorities [99, 1, 50] out of order. introspectExecuteOrder() returned ['test-early', 'test-mid', 'test-late'] (priority-ascending). listHooks()[0].runOrder matches. introspectExecuteOrder('does-not-exist') returns []. listAllHooks() zero-sub entries carry empty runOrder:[]. execute()'s own sort (line 97 on register) was already priority-correct, so this is introspection-only — no behavioral risk.

userNote: Hook fires (INDEX_START, FILE_INDEXED, INDEX_COMPLETE) await sequentially inside the bootstrap path. FILE_INDEXED fires per-file inside a for loop with await; for a 1000-file project that is 1000 sequential awaits even if no subscribers are registered. The per-iteration overhead is small but real (Promise allocation + microtask flush). Consider skipping execute() when subscriber count is zero, or batching.
actionsTaken:
Measurement-first ticket. The userNote suggested two possible fixes (skip-when-empty or batched) — Wave 2B was asked to measure first, then decide.

MEASUREMENT — instrumented hookRegistry.execute() with a monkey-patched timing wrapper that accumulated FILE_INDEXED / INDEX_START / INDEX_COMPLETE durations during a real bootstrap against /home/user/st8 (281 source files, indexer ends up firing FILE_INDEXED 283 times including .md). Two runs:

  BEFORE any change:
    FILE_INDEXED:   283 fires, 0.82 ms total, 0.0029 ms/fire
    INDEX_START:    9.36 ms (one fire, dominated by sonic-daemon spawn attempt)
    INDEX_COMPLETE: 1683.61 ms (one fire, dominated by emitter/gap/intent/force-checks)

  AFTER fast path landed:
    FILE_INDEXED:   283 fires, 0.76 ms total, 0.0027 ms/fire
    INDEX_START:    6.75 ms
    INDEX_COMPLETE: 1693.01 ms

DECISION — total bootstrap FILE_INDEXED cost (0.82 ms) is two orders of magnitude below the 500 ms threshold the brief set for switching to batched/Promise.all. Keep the per-file fire pattern. Rationale beyond the threshold: per-file granularity is the founder's stated intent (HOOK-ARCHITECTURE §6 — 'identification built into the indexer'), and a future per-file subscriber (Louis lock checks, real-time UI mirror) needs the per-file boundary to do its work; batching would force every such subscriber to re-implement the iteration.

SMALL OPTIMIZATION — added a zero-subscriber fast path at the top of HookRegistry.execute(): when `_hooks.get(name)` is empty AND `listenerCount(name)` is zero, return the empty summary immediately (still async-wrapped so callers can await unchanged). Both checks are authoritative — if both are zero, nothing observable happens, so skipping the loop + EventEmitter dispatch + try/catch is provably side-effect-free. Verified microbench shows ~15% reduction in per-call overhead (1.93 µs → 1.61 µs) on a fresh registry; whole-bootstrap improvement is small because the constant is already tiny.

NO NEW HOOK CONSTANT added (would have been FILES_INDEXED for the batched alternative). The 'wait for a real subscriber to drive the contract' guidance from earlier Wave 2A tickets applies — premature batching seeds the API with assumptions a future subscriber may not share.

Verified end-to-end: `node start.js .` boots to '[st8] Ready!' with [st8:force-check] 5/6 checks pass (baseline preserved — FC4 gap-analyzer is Wave 2D's pre-existing failure). Persistence tests remain 10/10.
residualConcerns:
If a future subscriber registers expensive synchronous work on FILE_INDEXED (e.g. AST re-parse), the 0.0027 ms/fire baseline becomes irrelevant and the measurement should be re-run. The fast path only helps the zero-subscriber case; with one subscriber it does nothing.

The userNote also mentioned 'consider batching.' I rejected batching for the reasons above (granularity is intentional, no current subscriber needs it), but if Louis-locking lands and its per-file work turns out to be I/O-bound, a FILES_INDEXED batched hook with the full file array could be added then. The fast path doesn't preclude that future addition.
reviewNote:
MEASUREMENT REPLICATED: ran my own microbench `for i in 0..1000: await execute(FILE_INDEXED, {})` against a fresh registry — 1000 fires in 1.00 ms (1.003 µs/fire), so 283 fires would be ~0.28 ms. Executor measured 0.82 ms on the full bootstrap (includes EventEmitter dispatch + verbose-disabled overhead). Same order of magnitude. Decision to keep per-file granularity is sound and well-reasoned (founder's intent per HOOK-ARCHITECTURE §6).

FAST PATH CODE REVIEW (hook-registry.js L120-128): short-circuit checks BOTH `arr.length === 0` AND `listenerCount(name) === 0` — correct, can't bail on only one. Returns the same `{ok, fail, errors}` shape as the normal path — verified via live probe: `r.execute(HOOKS.PRD_GENERATE, {})` on empty registry returns `{ok:0, fail:0, errors:[]}` without throwing. Verbose log path is preserved with explicit '0 subscribers — fast path' suffix so the trace is honest about which branch ran. Provably side-effect-free as the executor claims.

No behavioral risk; pure additive optimization.

userNote: All four INDEX_COMPLETE subscribers swallow errors and continue. A failure in manifest-generator (P=10) does NOT prevent schema-card-emitter (P=20) from running, even though emitter depends on the manifest's outputs in the gap-analyzer/force-check chain. This is per-design isolation, but the consequence is a 'partial run' where downstream subscribers operate on stale or missing inputs. Consider a fail-fast mode for development.
actionsTaken: INDEX_COMPLETE subscriber errors are now aggregated and surfaced in two places: (1) main.js captures the execute() summary, logs a per-source breakdown of any subscriber errors with `[st8] INDEX_COMPLETE finished with N subscriber error(s):` followed by `[st8]   - <source>: <error>` lines and a pointer to the JSON dump. (2) _writeIndexCompleteSummary writes a structured JSON report to `.st8/index-complete-errors.json` with {timestamp, ok, fail, errors: [{source, error}]}. The file is overwritten every INDEX_COMPLETE pass — a clean run truncates the previous run's errors. CI / dashboards / future force-checks integrations have a machine-readable surface for subscriber failures. Subscriber isolation is preserved — later subscribers still run after an earlier throw. What changed is visibility: errors are no longer scrollback-only. main.js now exports {main, _applyFileChange, _writeIndexCompleteSummary} and only auto-runs main() when invoked as an entry point (require.main === module). Tests: writer shape; truncate-on-clean-run; end-to-end registry aggregation of multiple thrown subscribers; the integration test feeds an execute() summary through the writer.
residualConcerns: No fail-fast mode added (still listed as roadmap P3). A subscriber throw still logs and continues — the change is visibility, not behavior change. If a future development run wants 'throw aborts the chain', the roadmap item is the right place for it. The JSON dump is overwritten each run — there is no history. A future CI integration that wants timelines should snapshot the file per build.

userNote: POST /api/record-commit and POST /api/tickets have no authentication. The post-commit shell hook posts from localhost, but the server binds to 0.0.0.0 by default in some configs. A remote attacker could pollute activity_log with arbitrary COMMIT_RECORDED entries or create fake tickets. Bind to 127.0.0.1 by default, or add an origin/host check on these write routes.
actionsTaken:
Implemented shared-secret auth on the two write routes that fire hooks.

New module src/core/server/auth.js:
  - ensureSecret(targetDir): on first call generates 32 random bytes via crypto.randomBytes, writes <targetDir>/.st8/server.secret as 64 hex chars with mode 0600 via atomic tmp+rename; idempotent on subsequent calls (reads back the existing secret). Throws on missing targetDir (silent fallback would be a cheat — auth cannot operate without a known secret).
  - readSecret(targetDir): returns null on missing dir / missing file / too-short content (<16 chars).
  - safeEqual(a, b): crypto.timingSafeEqual after length check; rejects non-string inputs without throwing.
  - checkRequest(req, targetDir): returns {ok:true} or {ok:false, status, reason}. Statuses: 503 no-secret-on-disk, 401 missing-header, 401 wrong-secret. Reason is for server-side logging only — the client sees a generic "unauthorized".
  - isLoopback(req): accepts 127.0.0.1, ::1, ::ffff:127.0.0.1.

Server wiring (src/core/server/app.js):
  - listen() callback now calls _ensureAuthSecret() right after _writePortFile(). Failure logs a warning and continues — read-only endpoints still work; auth-required POSTs return 503 until a secret is on disk.
  - CORS Access-Control-Allow-Headers extended with X-St8-Secret so browser fetch() preflights do not strip it.
  - _handleRecordCommit and _handleTickets POST gate on auth.checkRequest BEFORE parsing body. GET /api/tickets is unauthenticated by design (read-only).
  - New route GET /api/auth-token, loopback-gated via auth.isLoopback(req). Returns {secret:<hex>} to localhost callers so the frontend can fetch it on page load. Non-loopback → 403. Non-GET → 405.

Post-commit shell hook (scripts/git-hooks/post-commit):
  - REPO_ROOT resolution hoisted out of the ST8_PORT conditional so it is available for the new secret lookup.
  - Reads .st8/server.secret if present; passes X-St8-Secret: <secret> on the curl POST. Missing file → curls without the header (server 401/503; commit still succeeds — hook is fire-and-forget by design).

Frontend (src/frontend/app.js):
  - Module-load bootstrap: GET /api/auth-token at page load, cached as window._st8SecretPromise.
  - New window.st8AuthFetch(url, opts) wrapper attaches X-St8-Secret once the Promise resolves.
  - window.makeTicketFromNotes() switched from raw fetch() to st8AuthFetch().

Tests:
  tests/core/server/auth.test.js (15 unit probes): ensureSecret generates a 64-hex secret with mode 0600 and is idempotent; readSecret null-cases; safeEqual equal/unequal/length-mismatch/non-string; checkRequest covers all four return shapes including the 503 no-secret-on-disk; isLoopback accepts the three canonical loopback addresses and rejects external + missing-socket.
  tests/core/server/app-auth-routes.test.js (10 integration probes against real St8Server on ephemeral ports): boot creates .st8/server.secret; POST /api/record-commit with no/wrong/correct header returns 401/401/200; POST /api/tickets same shape (pre-populates file_registry to satisfy the fingerprint FK); GET /api/tickets remains unauthenticated; GET /api/auth-token returns secret to loopback caller and matches readSecret; non-GET on /api/auth-token returns 405.

Live HTTP smoke (port 4949):
  no header   → {"error":"unauthorized"} status 401
  wrong       → {"error":"unauthorized"} status 401
  right       → {"ok":true,"hash":"livetest"} status 200
  auth-token  → {"secret":"<64 hex>"} (loopback)
residualConcerns:
Three real follow-ups:
  1. Token rotation is not implemented. ensureSecret is one-shot — to rotate, delete .st8/server.secret and restart the server (then every external caller, including the post-commit hook and any open browser tab, must re-read the new value). A follow-up ticket could add POST /api/auth-token (loopback-gated + current-secret-gated) that regenerates.
  2. The frontend bootstrap fetches /api/auth-token unconditionally on page load. If the server is down at page load, the Promise resolves to null and subsequent POSTs go without the header (server returns 401 — visible failure). No retry/refresh loop. Acceptable for the current frontend lifetime (page reload re-fetches), but a long-running SPA session would benefit from a refresh-on-401.
  3. GET /api/auth-token is loopback-gated but NOT secret-gated, on the rationale that the frontend has no prior secret to authenticate with. For a stricter posture an alternative is to require the user to copy the secret from .st8/server.secret manually (deferred — UX cost is too high for the threat model).

The Wave 2A residualConcerns about start.js shell:true and main.js missing SIGTERM are out of cluster scope (lifecycle cluster) but worth flagging again — a SIGTERM-killed server today leaves .st8/server.port stale; the same applies to .st8/server.secret if a future rotation policy ever depends on the file being unlinked on shutdown (currently it is not unlinked, which is the desired behaviour for restart-survivability).
reviewNote: Live HTTP probe on port 4949 (real main.js boot, real .st8/server.secret on disk): POST /api/record-commit returns 401/401/200 for missing/wrong/correct X-St8-Secret. POST /api/tickets matches the same shape (test coverage). GET /api/tickets remains unauthenticated (200 with no header) by design. GET /api/auth-token returns 200 over loopback; POST returns 405. Cheat-check passed: with the secret file removed, POST returns 503 ("no-secret-on-disk") not 200 — the missing-file path does NOT silently allow. auth.js uses crypto.timingSafeEqual (not ===) with length-prefix check that returns false before the timingSafeEqual call to avoid the buffer-length throw. ensureSecret writes via tmp+rename atomic; chmod 0600 belt-and-braces wraps the mode flag. Frontend (app.js:40) bootstraps window._st8SecretPromise on page load and st8AuthFetch attaches the header — makeTicketFromNotes uses the wrapper. residualConcerns honestly flags token rotation deferred and the loopback-only auth-token bootstrap as design choices, not gaps.

userNote: POST /api/record-commit accepts arbitrary `filesChanged` value type — the route does not validate that it's a number. The shell hook always produces a number, but anyone POSTing manually could pass a string/object/array and it lands in activity_log.details as-is. Tighten validation to {hash: string, filesChanged?: number, ...}.
actionsTaken:
Added a strict validator validateRecordCommitPayload(input) at module scope in src/core/server/app.js (exported so the unit test file can require it directly). _handleRecordCommit now calls the validator BEFORE invoking persistence — invalid payloads return 400 with a clear human-readable error.

Validation rules:
  - hash:         REQUIRED string, 1..200 chars
  - shortHash:    optional string, 0..40 chars   (default "" when missing)
  - subject:      optional string, 0..500 chars  (default "")
  - author:       optional string, 0..300 chars  (default "")
  - timestamp:    optional string, 0..50 chars   (default "")
  - branch:       optional string, 0..200 chars  (default "")
  - filesChanged: optional integer 0..10000      (default 0 when missing)

Strict mode: unknown keys → 400 with "unknown field: <name>". This prevents callers from polluting activity_log.details with attacker-controlled fields. Also: invalid JSON body → 400 with the parser's error message (was: thrown 500 from the inner JSON.parse).

The route now writes the NORMALIZED payload (defaults applied, extras stripped) to activity_log AND to the COMMIT_RECORDED hook context — subscribers see a well-typed shape every time, not whatever the client sent.

Tests (tests/core/server/validate-record-commit.test.js, 24 probes):
  Pure validator (19):
    - happy minimal {hash} + happy full canonical payload
    - non-object inputs (null, undefined, number, string, array, array of values)
    - missing hash; non-string hash (number, bool, array, object, null); empty hash; overlong hash
    - unknown field
    - non-string optional strings: 5 fields × 4 bad types (20 probes inside one test)
    - overlong optional string (subject 501 chars)
    - filesChanged: string "5" → 400, array → 400, object → 400, float 3.14 → 400, negative -1 → 400, NaN/Infinity/-Infinity → 400, above cap 10001 → 400
    - filesChanged: 0 and 10000 (MAX) accepted
    - null/undefined optional fields treated as omitted (defaults applied)
    - normalized payload is a fresh object (not the input)
  Integration HTTP (5, with auth header attached):
    - filesChanged="5" → 400 with "filesChanged must be a number"
    - filesChanged=[1,2,3] → 400 with same
    - unknown field "exploit" → 400 with field name
    - invalid JSON body → 400 with parser detail
    - happy path → 200 with {ok:true, hash}

Live HTTP smoke (port 5050) results matched every expected response.

Final cluster test suite: 74/74 passing (21 hook-registry + 5 persistence-shared + 15 auth unit + 10 auth integration + 24 validation = 75 listed; actual count 74 because two tests share a single t.test()).
residualConcerns:
_handleTickets POST validates only truthy fingerprint+filepath. A parallel strict validator (validateCreateTicketPayload) would catch wrong-type userNote/sha256Hash/identityBundle. Out of scope for this ticket (the userNote named only /api/record-commit's filesChanged). Natural follow-up alongside the auth+validation layer; consider a shared validation harness in src/core/server/validators.js once a third route needs the same shape.

FILES_CHANGED_MAX = 10000 is a sanity cap derived from anti-abuse intuition rather than a real upper bound on git commits (massive merges, monorepo-wide formatters can exceed 10000). If a legitimate workflow hits the cap, the post-commit hook will see a 400 and the commit will still succeed (hook is fire-and-forget). A future tuning could raise it or remove it entirely.

The shell hook emits filesChanged as a number via jq's --argjson; the awk fallback emits it inline as 0 which is shell-numeric. Both paths produce the canonical type the validator accepts. Manually crafted POSTs are the only way to trip the new validation today — which is the desired surface.
reviewNote: Live HTTP probe (with valid auth header attached): filesChanged="5" → 400, filesChanged=[1,2,3] → 400, unknown field "bogus" → 400, happy path filesChanged:3 → 200. The validator at app.js:75 (validateRecordCommitPayload) is exported for direct unit-testability and the route at app.js:1716 calls it BEFORE persistence work, so a malformed payload cannot reach activity_log or the hook context. Validator rules match the actionsTaken claim: hash required string 1..200, five optional strings each with their own length cap, filesChanged optional integer 0..10000 with explicit rejection of strings, arrays, objects, floats, negatives, NaN, ±Infinity, and >MAX. Unknown-key check fires before type checks so a typo or attacker-controlled key (e.g. "exploit": ...) returns 400 with the field name. The 400 path also wraps the inner JSON.parse so malformed bodies return 400 instead of 500. Normalized output is a fresh object — confirmed by `assert.notEqual(r.payload, input)` in validate-record-commit.test.js:180. residualConcerns honestly notes (a) _handleTickets POST still uses truthy-fingerprint+filepath checks rather than a parallel strict validator (real follow-up) and (b) FILES_CHANGED_MAX = 10000 is an anti-abuse heuristic that legitimate monorepo merges could trip — fire-and-forget hook absorbs the 400 without breaking the commit.
